Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Methods state 'HL1–4 (Genbank MF770512-770515)' genes were cloned previously; HL2 corresponds to MF770513.

Source_Species.reason: HL2 is described as a C. roseus hydrolase/tabersonine synthase throughout the paper.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species for HL2 is explicitly Catharanthus roseus.

Applied_Species.reason: HL2 function was tested using C. roseus-derived enzyme and also in C. roseus context/reactions.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Catharanthus roseus is the in planta species discussed for the pathway.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Little Delicata and Pacifica White are plant varieties used for metabolite quantification, but HL2 itself was not specifically tested in those two varieties.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: HL2 is called hydrolase 2/tabersonine synthase, and the paper states dehydrosecodine is cyclized by a number of α/β-hydrolases; Fig. 1 notes HL2 has also been named tabersonine synthase.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results show HL2 with VNS leads to formation of 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine in tobacco and in vitro assays.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These products are monoterpenoid indole alkaloids in the MIA pathway described throughout the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Vindolinines are quantified in leaves and flowers, but venalstonine accumulation site was shown from leaf chloroform extract; the exact combined site assignment for all HL2-linked terminal metabolites is not directly established.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper discusses MIA diversity and bioactivity generally, but does not directly assign a specific biological function to vindolinines/venalstonine in Results.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show HL2 cyclizes the VNS-generated intermediate to vindolinine epimers and venalstonine, and HL2 catalyzes reverse reaction from tabersonine/vincadifformine to secodine-type intermediates enabling these products.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are supported by Results: HL2 works with VNS for product formation, catalyzes reverse reaction, HL1 cannot substitute and HL3 is less active.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods were used in the Results for HL2-related claims: transient expression in N. benthamiana, recombinant in vitro assays, substrate feeding, and LC-MS/MS.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: HL2 is experimentally paired with VNS and DPAS; DPAS could be substituted with GS, linking HL2 functionally with GS as well.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These experimental contexts are explicitly described in Results.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The alias HL2/TabS is explicit; HL2 is used as cyclase, and homologs HL4/VmHL could substitute while HL1 could not.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a forward-looking application inference; heterologous co-expression feasibility is suggested by experiments but not explicitly claimed as breeding/engineering value in the paper.

Omics_Data.reason: The leaf epidermal transcriptome/EST dataset pertains to VNS identification, not HL2 in this paper; no HL2-specific omics data are presented here.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: In vitro, addition of VNS to DPAS+HL2 converted OAP away from tabersonine toward vindolinines/venalstonine, supporting flux diversion away from tabersonine under VNS-active conditions.

corrections.reason: Little Delicata and Pacifica White are plant varieties used for metabolite quantification, but HL2 itself was not specifically tested in those two varieties.

corrections.old_value: Leaf epidermal transcriptome/EST dataset cited; accession NA
corrections.reason: The leaf epidermal transcriptome/EST dataset pertains to VNS identification, not HL2 in this paper; no HL2-specific omics data are presented here.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Methods explicitly list DPAS as Genbank KU865331.

Source_Species.reason: DPAS used in the study is from Catharanthus roseus.

Applied_Species.reason: DPAS was applied in pathway reconstruction relevant to C. roseus metabolism.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Catharanthus roseus is the species context of the pathway.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Little Delicata and Pacifica White were used for metabolite profiling, but DPAS itself was not specifically tested by variety.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: DPAS is named dihydroprecondylocarpine acetate synthase and is described as catalyzing an NADPH-dependent reduction.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: DPAS participates in assays yielding 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine via the dehydrosecodine intermediate.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Vindolinines were quantified in leaves and flowers, but this field attributes terminal metabolite accumulation directly to DPAS and includes venalstonine without direct flower evidence.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: General statements on MIA diversity are present, but not a direct Results-based functional assignment for these products.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper states reduction of OAP by DPAS generates ring-opened dehydrosecodine, and addition of VNS diverts this intermediate toward vindolinine/venalstonine biosynthesis.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summarized points are supported by the described transient expression and in vitro experiments, including formation of dihydrovindolinines without HL2.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods were indeed used for DPAS-containing assays.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: DPAS is experimentally used with VNS and HL2; GS can substitute for DPAS, so functional relationship with GS is supported.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Results and Methods explicitly describe NADPH-dependent in vitro assays and OAP substrate feeding/usage.

Other_Important_Info.reason: DPAS acts as an upstream helper enzyme in these experiments and is not the main discovery focus of the paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Pathway reconstruction implication is reasonable from the data but not explicitly stated as an engineering application in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: DPAS feeds the shared dehydrosecodine pool that can go to tabersonine/catharanthine or, with VNS/HL2, toward vindolinines, so competing branch support is consistent with Results and pathway model.

corrections.reason: Little Delicata and Pacifica White were used for metabolite profiling, but DPAS itself was not specifically tested by variety.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results explicitly identify VNS as Genbank OL677442.

Source_Species.reason: VNS was identified from Catharanthus roseus leaf epidermal transcriptome.

Applied_Species.reason: VNS was tested in planta in C. roseus and in heterologous systems for C. roseus pathway reconstruction.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Catharanthus roseus is the in planta species for VIGS and expression context.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: VIGS and epidermal RNA work used Little Delicata, and metabolite profiling included Pacifica White, but VNS functional testing was not directly shown in Pacifica White.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper explicitly names VNS as vindolinine synthase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: VNS is explicitly identified as a Fe(II)/α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase, and sequence alignment showed HXDXnH and YXnRXS motifs.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: VNS is placed in monoterpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis for vindolinine/venalstonine formation.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show VNS diverts the dehydrosecodine intermediate away from tabersonine toward alternatively cyclized products.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper presents VNS as the enzyme that diverts a shared intermediate at a branch point.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results and Discussion indicate VNS most likely acts on dehydrosecodine generated by DPAS or by HL2 reverse reaction from tabersonine.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper proposes VNS forms a radical intermediate that is further cyclized by HL2/HL4 to 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This description matches the Results and Discussion: a highly unusual redox-neutral reaction requiring α-ketoglutarate, O2, and ascorbate that diverts dehydrosecodine toward vindolinines/venalstonine.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: VNS clearly diverts flux toward the target products.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: VNS is clearly a key branch-switch enzyme, but the paper does not explicitly establish it as rate-limiting.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: VIGS shows requirement in planta, and transient/in vitro assays show sufficiency with partners to redirect flux from tabersonine to vindolinines/venalstonine.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: These are the explicit products linked to VNS in vivo/in vitro.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Vindolinines are documented in leaves and flowers, but venalstonine was shown from leaf chloroform extract only; the combined site statement is not fully supported for all listed metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper discusses chemical diversity and bioactivity generally, but not a direct demonstrated function for these metabolites in Results.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results explicitly state VNS has EST 83 in epidermal dataset, is enriched in leaf epidermis versus whole leaf, and is detected in flowers, roots, and stems.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This restates the observed expression pattern in C. roseus.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Table 1 and D2O experiments support α-ketoglutarate/ascorbate requirement, Fe(II) stimulation, and +1 deuterium incorporation.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each numbered point is directly supported by Results, including identification, VIGS phenotype, pathway redirection, unusual chemistry/cofactor dependence, and hydrolase substitution specificity.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches were used and reported in Results/Methods.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: VNS was tested with HL2, HL4, DPAS, GS, and VmHL in assays.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results note strong vindolinine reduction upon VNS silencing and small changes in catharanthine/vindoline, indicating flux redistribution.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Metabolic engineering relevance is inferable, and heterologous reconstruction was shown, but the explicit application claims about engineering target/stacking and tag avoidance are partly extrapolative.

Omics_Data.reason: A leaf epidermal transcriptome/EST dataset is explicitly used for VNS identification, and source/raw data availability is stated; no public transcriptome accession is given.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results/Discussion support likely action on dehydrosecodine rather than OAP/tabersonine directly, near-loss of activity with C-terminal His tag, and the closest Vinca minor homolog at 48% identity with no homolog >50% in surveyed non-vindolinine species.
